title: Aztec
description: Aztec is a privacy-first zkRollup Layer 2 on Ethereum where smart contracts can have both private and public state and functions. Private functions execute locally in the user's Private Execution Environment (PXE) and generate client-side zero-knowledge proofs. Public functions execute on the sequencer like an EVM L2. Private state is held as encrypted note commitments with nullifiers. Public state is an account-based store. Contracts are written in Noir.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Aztec's private state lives as encrypted UTXO-style notes that only the holder of the decryption key can read, so balances stay hidden from outside observers. Transfer amounts are concealed too — spending a $5 note on a $3.50 purchase nullifies it and emits two new notes ($1.50 to sender, $3.50 to recipient), so only sender and recipient know the transfer. Confidentiality holds only inside the private domain: arguments passed from a private function into a public one are publicly visible.

citations.cited_text: Private state is encrypted and owned by users who hold the decryption keys. It uses an append-only data structure since updating records directly would leak information about the transaction graph.
Notes work like cash. To spend a 5 dollar note on a $3.50 purchase, you nullify the $5 note and create two new notes: $1.50 for yourself and $3.50 for the recipient.
Passing arguments to a public function from a private function. All of those arguments will be publicly visible.

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Private transfers move encrypted notes between accounts — observers see only commitments and nullifiers, and nullifiers cannot be linked to state records without the owner's keys. The commitment tree hides the underlying notes, so both counterparties stay inside the shielded set. Leakage arises when a private function enqueues a public call: the public sender is then visible. Watching the L2 transaction pool also exposes per-transaction fingerprints that can be linked to user behaviour.

citations.cited_text: The nullifier is computed such that observers cannot link a state record to its nullifier without the owner's keys.
Rather than storing entire notes, the protocol stores note commitments (hashes) in a Merkle tree called the note hash tree.
Calling a public function from a private function and revealing the msg_sender of that call (the msg_sender will be publicly visible).
transactions with distinctive fingerprints can be linked to specific contracts or to patterns of user behavior.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: Asset identity is hidden for transfers that stay inside the private state model. Private state takes the form of notes — encrypted data that only the owner can decrypt — so any asset identifier and amount fields the contract puts inside the note are hidden. Only commitment hashes appear on-chain, leaving the asset type invisible to observers. Boundary interactions with specific public token contracts (deposits and withdrawals crossing into public state) remain observable at that interface.

citations.cited_text: . Notes are encrypted pieces of data that only their owner can decrypt.
Rather than storing entire notes, the protocol stores note commitments (hashes) in a Merkle tree called the note hash tree.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Aztec operates as a separate zkRollup connected to Ethereum with its own transaction format and sequencer participation, so submitting an Aztec transaction is itself an observable, distinct action rather than something hidden within ordinary L1 activity. No mechanism exists to disguise usage as a plain native transfer — entry into the system is a recognisable interaction with Aztec infrastructure.

citations.cited_text: Alpha is connected to Ethereum mainnet and supports user transactions. Governance and staking infrastructure are fully operational. This network requires real stakes for sequencer participation.

properties.name: Time-to-finality
properties.notes: An Aztec transaction is final when its epoch proof is verified on L1. A single proof can cover one checkpoint (1m 12s) up to one epoch (38m 24s = 2,304 seconds), and the epoch root proof is verified by the HonkVerifier contract before the proven checkpoint is advanced. Worst-case for inclusion is one full epoch — Ethereum L1 finality (~13 minutes) sits on top before the proof transaction itself is irreversible.

citations.cited_text: Each epoch root proof is verified by the HonkVerifier smart contract on Ethereum before the proven checkpoint number is advanced and the epoch outbox state root is inserted into the Outbox.
a single proof can cover one Checkpoint (1m 12s) to one epoch (38m 24s).
Once the epoch proof is verified, the state transitions are considered final and the private transfer has settled.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: Aztec accounts hold three independently stored secrets: a master nullifier secret, a master incoming viewing secret, and an application-defined signing key. Protocol keys are generated by the PXE on account creation on the Grumpkin curve. App-siloed nullifier keys are deterministically derived from the master nullifier key so they do not add to the count. The signing key is a separate secret because it is abstracted to the account contract developer and is not derivable from the protocol keys.

citations.cited_text:
Nullifier Keys Spending notes (destroying private state) Yes No
Incoming Viewing Keys Decrypting received notes Yes No
Signing Keys Transaction authorization No (app-defined) Yes
Protocol keys (nullifier and incoming viewing) are automatically generated by the Private Execution Environment (PXE) when creating an account.
For each application, an app-siloed key is derived: nhk_app = hash(nhk_m, app_contract_address)
The signing key is abstracted to the account contract developer, allowing complete flexibility in authentication methods.

properties.name: Deposit time
properties.notes: Deposits into Aztec take roughly one checkpoint after the L1 transaction is included. The L1 Inbox exposes sendL2Message() to send a message from L1 to L2, and the message becomes available on L2 once the L1 block is processed by the rollup. A one-checkpoint lag applies before consumption to prevent sequencer DOS attacks. Checkpoint cadence is roughly 1m 12s, giving a typical wait of ~72 seconds with no longer queue or time-lock.

citations.cited_text:
sendL2Message() ​ 

Sends a message from L1 to L2.
The message becomes available on L2 once the L1 block is processed by the rollup
empty because there has to be a 1 checkpoint lag to prevent sequencer DOS attacks
a single proof can cover one Checkpoint (1m 12s) to one epoch (38m 24s).

properties.name: Withdraw time
properties.notes: Withdrawing from Aztec waits for the active epoch to end, the epoch proof to be generated, and that proof to be verified on Ethereum before the outbox root is available. A single proof covers one checkpoint up to one epoch (38m 24s = 2,304 s), and the proof-submission window is 1h 16m (4,560 s) before unproven checkpoints are pruned. Once verified there is no challenge period — finality on L1 is immediate. Typical worst-case sums to one epoch plus the proving window — 6,864 s (~1h 54m).

citations.cited_text:
Must wait for the epoch to end

Must wait for the proof to be generated

Must wait for proof verification on Ethereum

Only then can tokens be claimed on L1
a single proof can cover one Checkpoint (1m 12s) to one epoch (38m 24s). Unproven checkpoints are pruned after the proof submission window of 1h 16m expires.
There is no challenge period; finality on L1 is immediate once the proof is verified.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: Aztec's sequencer set is permissionless — joining requires staking 200K AZTEC — and each epoch samples a 48-member committee from the active set of 3,548 sequencers, so no single party holds inclusion authority. If the sampled committee censors, anyone bonding 332M AZTEC joins an escape-hatch candidate set, and every 2d 23h a candidate is pseudo-randomly selected to propose and prove checkpoints autonomously. Inclusion is probabilistic rather than guaranteed within a fixed deadline.

citations.cited_text: Joining the sequencer set is permissionless and requires staking 200 K AZTEC. For each epoch, the rollup samples a 48-member committee from the active sequencer set of 3548
anyone who bonds 332 M AZTEC will join the escape hatch candidate set. Every 2d 23h, a candidate is pseudo-randomly selected to propose and prove checkpoints fully autonomously.
All censorship resistance tools that are part of the protocol rely on probabilistic inclusion.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Aztec's sequencer and prover networks are part of the protocol itself rather than an external service: anyone can join the permissionless sequencer set by staking 200K AZTEC, and proving is permissionless too. The only external dependency is Ethereum L1, which provides data availability (EIP-4844 blobs whose commitments are revalidated by the epoch proof) and proof verification. No additional off-protocol committee, oracle, or relayer network is required for transfers.

citations.cited_text: Joining the sequencer set is permissionless and requires staking 200 K AZTEC. For each epoch, the rollup samples a 48-member committee from the active sequencer set of 3548
, and a single proof can cover one Checkpoint (1m 12s) to one epoch (38m 24s).
Each checkpoint proposal includes EIP-4844 blob commitments that are checked against the blob hashes in the proposing transaction.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.value: Can exit in a time period
properties.notes: Users can exit through a censorship-resistant fallback, but inclusion is probabilistic rather than guaranteed within a fixed deadline. If sampled committees censor, anyone who bonds 332M AZTEC joins an escape-hatch candidate set, and every 2d 23h a candidate is pseudo-randomly selected to propose and prove checkpoints autonomously. Users have 10 days to exit funds against an unwanted upgrade, with a 1-month upgrade delay, and withdrawal inclusion is simulated to take up to 20 days.

citations.cited_text: anyone who bonds 332 M AZTEC will join the escape hatch candidate set. Every 2d 23h, a candidate is pseudo-randomly selected to propose and prove checkpoints fully autonomously.
Users have 10d to exit funds in case of an unwanted upgrade. There is a 1mo delay before an upgrade is applied, and withdrawal inclusion
The project passes the walkaway test : users can exit in the presence of malicious operators even if the Security Council disappears.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: Upgrades are governed by an on-chain DAO where AZTEC token holders vote on payloads after sequencer signaling. Block producers signal during assigned slots — once quorum is reached anyone can submit a proposal, holders vote with snapshotted power, and execution triggers after a delay. The Rollup, its verifier, and messaging contracts are immutable, and Governance can designate a new Rollup address as canonical with a delay of roughly one month and ten days.

citations.cited_text: Signaling : Block producers on the canonical rollup signal support for a payload by calling signal() on the Governance Proposer during their assigned slots
, its verifier and its canonical messaging contracts cannot be changed. However, Governance owns critical permissions for configuration parameters
Total standard delay from proposal to execution: 1mo 10d

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: Proof generation for private functions runs entirely on the user's device. The PXE generates proofs of private function execution and sends them along with public function execution requests to the sequencer, and private inputs never leave the client-side PXE. The PXE is a TypeScript library that can run inside Node.js, wallet software or a browser, producing zero-knowledge proofs of correct execution before submitting note commitments and nullifiers to the sequencer.

citations.cited_text: The PXE generates proofs of private function execution, and sends these proofs along with public function execution requests to the sequencer. Private inputs never leave the client-side PXE.
It is a TypeScript library that can be run within Node.js, inside wallet software or a browser.

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: Private execution and proof generation happen client-side in the PXE, so plaintext inputs never reach the sequencer or prover network. Third-party node operators can still observe user-interest patterns — when the PXE queries the node for world state, the node learns which data the user is interested in, a known tradeoff users can mitigate by running their own node. Revealing a note's leaf index to a third party trivially reveals exactly the notes the user is about to read.

citations.cited_text: Private inputs never leave the client-side PXE.
When the PXE queries the node for world state (e.g., to check if a nullifier exists), the node learns which data the user is interested in.
Revealing the leaf's index to a third party trivially reveals exactly the note(s) you're about to read.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: The Aztec Ignition Chain went live in November 2025 as the first decentralized L2 on Ethereum, serving as the coordination layer that the execution layer sits on top of. The user-facing Alpha network runs at 1TPS with ~6s block times. Alpha is live but early — bugs, including critical ones, are expected — and new releases of the alpha network are issued regularly to address security vulnerabilities. Mainnet tenure is well under one year.

citations.cited_text: In November 2025, the Aztec Ignition Chain went live as the first decentralized L2 on Ethereum, it's the coordination layer that the execution layer sits on top of.

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Aztec's proving stack relies on classical elliptic-curve cryptography that is broken by Shor's algorithm. State validation uses SNARKs — succinct zero-knowledge proofs requiring a trusted setup — and L1 verification is performed by a HonkVerifier contract running Barretenberg's UltraHonk. The PLONK family relies on pairing-based elliptic-curve assumptions that do not survive a quantum adversary, and squisher circuits compress proofs into Standard Plonk or Fflonk with no lattice fallback.

citations.cited_text: SNARKs are succinct zero knowledge proofs that ensure state correctness, but require trusted setup

PROVER

Barretenberg 

Trusted Setups

Plonk: UltraHonk

Onchain verifier

HonkVerifier
we'll need some extra circuit(s) to squish a Honk proof (as produced by the Root Rollup Circuit) into a Standard Plonk or Fflonk proof, for cheap verification on Ethereum.

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: No compliance enforcement exists at any layer of the Aztec protocol. The protocol provides tools for developers to build private smart contracts with no blocklist, allowlist, or screening gate at the chain or app layer. Any compliance behaviour would need to be added by individual contract developers at the asset or app layer — the base protocol is a permissionless system enabling interaction with the public world through L2 and L1 functions without restriction filters.

citations.cited_text: Aztec provides a set of tools to enable developers to build private smart contracts.
Since Aztec also enables interaction with the public world (public L2 functions and L1 functions), private information can be accidentally leaked if developers aren't careful.

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: No entity enforces compliance restrictions at the Aztec protocol level. The protocol centres on private smart contracts where state variables are stored encrypted, and private functions execute without the world knowing which function was executed — leaving no chokepoint where an admin, governance body, third party, or asset issuer screens transfers. Governance contracts on L1 control protocol upgrades and parameters rather than user-level transfer enforcement.

citations.cited_text: Store state variables in an encrypted form, so that no one can see what those variables are, except those with the decryption key.
Execute a private function without the world knowing which function you've executed.

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: No protocol-level compliance gating exists on Aztec — no blocklist or allowlist checks, no KYC gate, no oracle-delivered policy attestations. The available primitive for voluntary compliance is selective disclosure via incoming viewing keys: the sender encrypts to the recipient's public viewing key, and the holder of the secret viewing key can decrypt. Sharing viewing access without compromising spending ability is a user-side action rather than an enforced gate.

citations.cited_text: The sender uses the recipient's public viewing key ( Ivpk ) to encrypt notes, and the recipient uses their secret viewing key ( ivsk ) to decrypt them.
Selective disclosure : Share viewing access without compromising spending ability

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: No protocol-level compliance gate is enforced at any stage of the private transfer lifecycle on Aztec. The privacy docs cover leakage across the private/public boundary, transaction timing, function fingerprints, and data queries, but never reference screening, allowlists, blocklists, or sanctions checks at deposit, transfer, or withdrawal paths. Interaction with public L2 and L1 functions is left to developer discretion, with no compliance hook bound to bridging or transfer flows.

citations.cited_text: Aztec provides a set of tools to enable developers to build private smart contracts.
Since Aztec also enables interaction with the public world (public L2 functions and L1 functions), private information can be accidentally leaked if developers aren't careful.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Aztec accounts include an incoming viewing key pair where the recipient decrypts notes encrypted to their public viewing key, giving the holder self-view over received notes. An owner can hand a viewing key to an auditor or accountant to grant read access without conferring spend authority. No admin, regulator, or governance role with mandatory read access into user notes exists in the key architecture.

citations.cited_text: The sender uses the recipient's public viewing key ( Ivpk ) to encrypt notes, and the recipient uses their secret viewing key ( ivsk ) to decrypt them.
Selective disclosure : Share viewing access without compromising spending ability

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: Viewing access on Aztec is fixed at account creation: incoming viewing keys are protocol-managed and not rotatable, and the sender encrypts with the recipient's public viewing key. Because protocol keys cannot be rotated and are embedded in the immutable address, sharing the secret viewing key with a third party hands over decryption rights to all past and future notes with no revocation path. There is no granular grant/revoke mechanism for retroactive auditor access or time-bounded disclosure.

citations.cited_text: Incoming Viewing Keys Decrypting received notes Yes No
Protocol keys (nullifier, incoming viewing): Cannot be rotated. They are embedded in the address, which is immutable. If compromised, a new account must be deployed.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Aztec enforces state-transition correctness through recursive SNARKs. The rollup circuits compress thousands of transactions into a single SNARK proof for verification on Ethereum, aggregating proofs from private kernel and AVM execution. Each epoch root proof is verified by the HonkVerifier contract on Ethereum before the proven checkpoint is advanced. Each state update must carry a ZK proof that the new state was derived by correctly applying valid user transactions.

citations.cited_text: The rollup circuits compress thousands of transactions into a single SNARK proof for verification on Ethereum.
Each epoch root proof is verified by the HonkVerifier smart contract on Ethereum before the proven checkpoint number is advanced
Each update to the system state must be accompanied by a ZK proof that ensures that the new state was derived by correctly applying a series of valid user transactions to the previous state.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: The Aztec monorepo houses all protocol components — the Barretenberg ZK prover backend (which also houses the Aztec VM), Solidity L1 rollup contracts, Noir code for Aztec contracts and protocol circuits, and TypeScript client and backend code — under a single repository. The codebase is released under the Apache License, Version 2.0, an OSI-approved open source licence covering the prover, contracts, circuits, and SDK together.

citations.cited_text: : Solidity code for the Ethereum contracts that process rollups
Apache License Version 2.0, January 2004 http://www.apache.org/licenses/ TERMS AND CONDITIONS FOR USE, REPRODUCTION, AND DISTRIBUTION

properties.name: Private State Scalability
properties.notes: Aztec's private state lives in two append-only Merkle trees — a note hash tree for commitments and a nullifier tree for spent notes — both accumulating leaves monotonically with usage. Private state uses an append-only data structure since updating records directly would leak information about the transaction graph, and modifying state means nullifying the existing record and creating a new one. Every transfer therefore adds at least one note commitment plus one nullifier, with no pruning path.

citations.cited_text: Private state is encrypted and owned by users who hold the decryption keys. It uses an append-only data structure since updating records directly would leak information about the transaction graph.
Modifying state is accomplished by nullifying the existing record and creating a new one.
Since updating state directly leaks information, we simulate updates by "destroying" old UTXOs and creating new ones, resulting in an append-only merkle tree.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: The PXE performs targeted tag-based lookups rather than scanning every log. The contract calls fetchTaggedLogs, and the PXE computes tags for every (sender, recipient) pair it knows about and queries the node for matching logs. Discovery is bounded by a sliding window with scanning extending from the aged index to a short range beyond the finalized index. Coverage is limited to registered counterparties: the PXE must know the sender's address in advance to compute the shared tagging secret.

citations.cited_text: The PXE computes tags for every (sender, recipient) pair it knows about, queries the node for matching logs, and returns them to the contract.
The PXE doesn't scan all possible tag indexes — it uses a window-based approach to efficiently find new logs
It scans from the aged index to 20 indexes beyond the finalized index, covering both recent and in-flight logs.
To discover notes from a particular sender, the recipient's PXE must know the sender's address in advance so it can compute the shared tagging secret.

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Aztec's private state follows a UTXO model: notes are encrypted pieces of data that only their owner can decrypt. Commitments live in an append-only Merkle structure and users prove they know the note preimage when updating private state. Spending consumes a record by publishing a nullifier — an associated nullifier is added to a nullifier set, computed so observers cannot link it to a state record without the owner's keys. Public state is account-based.

citations.cited_text: . Notes are encrypted pieces of data that only their owner can decrypt.
Users prove they know the note preimage when updating private state.
To "delete" private state, you add an associated nullifier to a nullifier set.

properties.name: Private Data Storage
properties.notes: Aztec's private state is anchored on-chain in the rollup. Note commitments (hashes) are stored in a Merkle tree called the note hash tree. Encrypted note bodies follow a hybrid delivery model — the standard onchain path emits an encrypted log as part of the transaction so recipients can find notes through note discovery, while an offchain path lets the sender share note data directly with a known recipient. Both routes resolve to the same on-chain commitment as the authoritative anchor.

citations.cited_text: Rather than storing entire notes, the protocol stores note commitments (hashes) in a Merkle tree called the note hash tree.
Onchain (encrypted logs): The standard method. Emit an encrypted log as part of your transaction. The encrypted note data is posted onchain
Offchain: If you know the recipient directly, share the note data with them. They store it in their PXE and can spend it later.

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.notes: Aztec is a general-purpose smart-contract rollup, so any DeFi application can be deployed on it. Contracts support both private and public state, with private functions able to enqueue public function calls within the same transaction, so the full DeFi surface (lending, AMMs, vaults) can be authored and used inside the rollup. External composability with Ethereum L1 DeFi is asynchronous via the Inbox/Outbox bridge rather than synchronous cross-call.

citations.cited_text: It supports smart contracts with both private & public state and private & public execution.
private functions executed by a transaction can enqueue public functions to be executed later in the transaction life cycle, but public functions cannot call private functions.
Private functions from other contracts can be called either regularly or statically by using self.call() and self.view()

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.value: Full programmability with public and private state
properties.notes: Aztec supports smart contracts with both private and public state and private and public execution, making it a general-purpose programmable environment rather than a payments-only system. Contracts are written in Noir, a zero-knowledge domain-specific language used for writing smart contracts for the Aztec network. A single transaction can flow from private client-side execution into public functions — private functions can enqueue public functions executed later in the same transaction.

citations.cited_text: It supports smart contracts with both private & public state and private & public execution.
Noir is a zero-knowledge domain specific language used for writing smart contracts for the Aztec network.
private functions executed by a transaction can enqueue public functions to be executed later in the transaction life cycle, but public functions cannot call private functions.
